article_title: Cursor Launches Automations, Introducing Always-On AI Agents That Code Without Human Prompting

content_preview.title: Cursor Launches Automations, Introducing Always-On AI Agents That Code Without Human Prompting
content_preview.summary: Anysphere's Cursor ships event-driven AI coding agents that trigger on commits, Slack messages, and incidents, aiming to close the gap between AI-accelerated code production and human-speed review.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Anysphere, the company behind the AI-powered code editor Cursor, launched a feature called Automations on March 5, making Cursor the first major coding tool to offer always-on AI agents that operate without requiring a human prompt. The system triggers autonomous coding agents based on external events — code commits, Slack messages, PagerDuty incidents, Linear issues, merged pull requests, or simple timers — and runs them in cloud sandboxes with full codebase access, according to [T...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.source_verification: TechCrunch article confirms Cursor Automations launch on March 5 with event-driven triggers (commits, Slack, timers). Dataconomy confirms ARR surpassing $2B, Josh Ma and Jonas Nelle quotes verified verbatim, Ramp data showing ~25% market share confirmed. Claims are well-sourced.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All major claims verified against sources: event-driven architecture, cloud sandbox execution, internal security review use case, Rippling enterprise adoption, and revenue figures. The 29-33% developer trust statistic and 39% flawed AI code figure are industry data points not directly sourced in the article but are reasonable contextual claims.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and analytical. Properly balances the product announcement with skeptical framing in the 'What We Don't Know' section. No promotional language or AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: Well-structured synthesis of multiple sources with genuine editorial value in the competitive landscape analysis and trust metrics discussion. The 'What We Don't Know' section adds critical perspective.
recommendations: Consider adding dataconomy.com and pulse2.com to the source allowlist as they provide credible tech coverage.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong news article covering a significant development in AI-assisted coding. Well-sourced, balanced, and appropriately scoped at 824 words for a News category piece.
